planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny position.

directions: Plant in free-draining soil and water while roots establish. Thin flowered stems after bloom to keep the low shrub compact. Carmine-pink buds open to soft pink flowers on gracefully arching branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this hybrid is among the hardier deutzias.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and water through the establishment year. Prune out old wood after flowering to maintain vigor. Dense upright clusters of pure white flowers cover the shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the back of a border where its size fits.

directions: Plant in ordinary well-drained soil and give this vigorous shrub ample space. Remove a portion of the oldest stems each year after bloom. Large panicles packed with double white flowers appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the richest flower color.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water deeply during dry spells. Thin the oldest canes after flowering to keep the shrub full. Deep carmine-pink flowers cover the rounded, bushy plant in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny mixed border.

directions: Plant in free-draining soil in full sun and keep watered while establishing. Renew by cutting out the oldest flowered stems after bloom. Pink flowers with deeper carmine reverses, resembling mountain laurel, arch along the branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a long-blooming species for warmer zones.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun or light shade and shelter from cold wind. Prune to shape after the main flush of flowers. Broad heads of small, starry white flowers open over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water during establishment. Thin some older stems after flowering to keep it open. Loose panicles of white flowers with grayish undersides appear in summer on the upright shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or dappled shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and give shelter from drying winds. Remove old flowered wood after bloom to renew the plant. Star-shaped white flowers flushed purple-pink on the outside open on slender arching stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the earliest deutzias to flower.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and water while establishing. Prune lightly after the early flowers fade. Comparatively large white flowers open on the bare or newly leafing branches in mid to late spring, ahead of most other deutzias.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny border.

directions: Plant in ordinary well-drained soil and keep watered the first year. Thin the oldest stems after flowering to maintain vigor. Flat clusters of small white, hawthorn-like flowers cover the shrub in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a later-flowering species.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. Prune after flowering to keep the bushy habit tidy. Flattened heads of fragrant white flowers, pink in bud, open in mid to late summer, later than most deutzias.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or partial shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water through establishment. Thin some of the oldest wood after flowering. Small starry white flowers stud the fine-textured, gray-green foliage in summer, and the arching stems suit a natural border.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, sunny site.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and protect from cold, drying winds in colder areas. Prune out old stems after the flowers fade. Large pink-tinged white flowers open in loose clusters in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tall flowering shrub.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and allow room for the upright, arching form. Remove a few of the oldest exfoliating stems after bloom. Fully double, pure white flowers crowd the branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low edging or foundation plant.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and keep the shallow roots mulched and moist. Shear lightly after flowering to hold the compact mound. Pink-flushed white flowers cover the low, spreading branches in mid to late spring, with burgundy fall foliage.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or fall while the ground can still be worked.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the surrounding soil and give it room for its long, cascading canes to arch and root where they touch ground. Prune out a third of the oldest stems right after flowering, since blooms form on the previous year's wood. Expect a sheet of golden-yellow flowers on bare branches in very early spring before the leaves unfurl.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or autumn in well-drained soil and full sun.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the root ball and twice as wide, then firm the soil and water in. Recognizable by its distinctly green four-angled stems that stay bright through winter. Prune after the greenish-yellow spring flowers fade to keep the upright habit tidy.

planting.note: Best planted in early spring or fall; this hybrid is grown from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Space plants to allow the full fountain of arching stems to develop and avoid shearing into a ball. The workhorse hybrid of suspensa and viridissima, it carries heavier bloom than either parent. Rejuvenate old, twiggy shrubs by cutting a third of the stems to the ground each year after flowering.

planting.note: Set out in early spring; its hardy flower buds suit colder gardens.

directions: Plant in a sunny, open spot with average, well-drained soil and water through the first season. Valued for flower buds that survive cold better than the border hybrids, making it one of the earliest and most reliable bloomers in the north. Amber-yellow flowers open a week or two ahead of most other forsythias.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and average soil.

directions: Give it a sunny position and unfussy, well-drained soil, then water until established. The only forsythia native to Europe, it forms a more upright, less arching shrub than the Asian species. Pale yellow flowers appear on bare stems in early spring; prune lightly after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for the earliest flowers of the genus.

directions: Site in full sun with room to arch and water through the first year. One of the very earliest forsythias, its soft primrose-yellow flowers can open in late winter during mild spells. Trim after flowering to shape and remove weak wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cold-climate gardens; buds tolerate hard winters.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with well-drained soil and keep evenly watered the first season. A cold-hardy northeastern-Asian species prized for reliable bud survival and good fall leaf color. Bright yellow flowers cover bare stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and moderate soil.

directions: Set in a sunny spot with average garden soil and water until roots take hold. A less common Japanese species with a compact, upright frame. Yellow bell-shaped flowers open in early spring ahead of the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in a sunny, open site.

directions: Give this vigorous shrub plenty of room to arch and avoid crowding it against other plants. One of the most heavily flowered older cultivars, smothering itself in deep yellow blooms. Renew by removing the oldest canes at ground level just after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set in a sunny bed and water through the first summer to establish. Bears a profusion of soft, pale sulfur-yellow flowers that light up gently rather than screaming. Prune spent wood after bloom to keep it well furnished to the base.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in full sun and average soil.

directions: Allow generous spacing so its large, upright frame can fill out naturally. Noted for unusually big, deep-yellow flowers with faint orange throats. Thin the oldest stems each spring after flowering to keep bloom heavy.

planting.note: Plant in spring; buds are hardier than most border types.

directions: Plant in full sun where you want dependable spring color and reddish fall foliage. Bred at the Arnold Arboretum for better bud hardiness and heavy, deep-gold flowering. Prune after bloom, removing a portion of the old canes yearly.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Site in full sun and give it room to develop its full, rounded shape. A German selection carrying rich golden-yellow flowers and dependable bud hardiness. Renew crowded plants by cutting the oldest third of stems after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; good for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot where its more compact frame fits tight borders and foundations. A smaller, densely twiggy selection that still flowers heavily in golden-yellow. Shape lightly right after the spring display.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest leaf variegation.

directions: Grow in full sun to keep the yellow-and-green variegated leaves vivid and the yellow stems bright. A dwarf, mounding selection valued as much for summer foliage as for spring flowers. Remove any all-green reverting shoots as soon as they appear.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set in full sun and enjoy its neat, upright habit that needs little corrective pruning. Sold as Week End, it flowers freely from top to bottom of each cane in bright yellow. Thin old wood after bloom to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; flower buds withstand very cold winters.

directions: Plant in full sun in northern gardens where bud-hardiness matters most. A prairie-bred cross that flowers reliably after winters that blast lesser forsythias. Golden-yellow blooms cover the bare stems in early spring; prune after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cold regions for hardy spring bloom.

directions: Give this large shrub a sunny, open spot and let it reach full size. A University of Minnesota introduction chosen for flower buds hardy well below zero. Prune old canes to the ground after the yellow spring flowers fade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in northern gardens; buds are extra cold-tolerant.

directions: Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and water through the first season. A Canadian selection bred for reliable flowering after harsh winters. Bright golden flowers open early in spring before the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy selection for cold northern sites.

directions: Set in a sunny bed with average soil and keep watered until established. Selected in New Hampshire for dependable bud hardiness and good burgundy fall color. Deep yellow flowers blanket the stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a low, hardy Canadian introduction.

directions: Plant in full sun where its low, spreading shape suits the front of a border. A compact, cold-hardy selection that flowers well in northern gardens. Trim lightly after the yellow spring bloom to keep it dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low groundcover or bank cover in full sun.

directions: Space plants across a slope or bed edge where the low, wide-spreading stems can knit into a mat. Sold as Gold Tide, it stays under knee height while blooming bright yellow. Shear lightly after flowering only if you need to contain the spread.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for densest flowering.

directions: Plant in full sun and enjoy its tidy, upright form that needs little pruning. Sold as Show Off, it packs flowers tightly along short internodes for a very dense display. Deep golden blooms coat the stems from base to tip in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; a compact selection for small spaces.

directions: Set in a sunny spot where its small, rounded frame fits containers, low borders, or foundations. Sold as Show Off Starlet, it flowers densely on a compact plant. Light yellow blooms line every stem in early spring; little pruning is needed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; dwarf habit suits pots and edges.

directions: Plant in full sun in a small bed or large container where a true dwarf forsythia is wanted. Sold as Sugar Baby, it stays low and rounded while still flowering heavily. Bright yellow blooms cover the little shrub in early spring with almost no pruning required.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low, mounded groundcover in full sun.

directions: Space these dwarf plants close together to form a low, dense mat over a bed or slope. A true miniature with fine twiggy stems and small leaves that flowers pale yellow. Shear only lightly after bloom, since heavy pruning spoils the neat cushion shape.

planting.note: Plant near a wall or bank so the long stems can trail and cascade.

directions: Site at the top of a wall or slope where its extremely long, lax stems can pour downward and root at the tips. The most pendulous form of weeping forsythia, effective trained over rocks or fences. Golden flowers stud the trailing branches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown as much for foliage as for flowers.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade where the netted leaves can be admired up close. Grown chiefly for green leaves boldly veined in creamy silver, with yellow spring flowers as a bonus. Prune lightly after bloom and remove any plain-green shoots that revert.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low groundcover; grown mainly for foliage.

directions: Space plants across a bank or bed where the low, wide-spreading stems can root down and cover ground. A dwarf, sparse-flowering selection valued for erosion control rather than heavy bloom. Expect only scattered greenish-yellow flowers, mainly on older plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to keep the leaf margins bright.

directions: Grow in full sun to hold the strong yellow leaf edges that fade in shade. A variegated selection offering yellow-edged foliage all summer plus the usual golden spring flowers. Cut out any all-green reverting branches promptly.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for a dense display.

directions: Set in a sunny spot and let its compact, upright frame fill in. Sold as Magical Gold, it flowers densely along the whole stem in bright yellow. Prune out older wood after bloom to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; a dwarf for small gardens.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed or container where a very small, rounded forsythia is needed. A compact Polish selection sold as Mikador, dense and slow-growing. Yellow flowers cover the little shrub in early spring with minimal pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun and average soil.

directions: Site in full sun where its softer color reads well against evergreens. An older selection bearing pale primrose-yellow flowers rather than harsh gold. Renew by removing the oldest canes just after the spring bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; a dwarf for tight spaces and hedging.

directions: Plant in full sun where its small, dense frame suits low hedges, edging, or containers. Sold as Citrus Swizzle, it stays compact and needs little shaping. Bright yellow flowers wrap the stems in early spring ahead of the leaves.

planting.note: Set out dormant or containerized plants in early spring or fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline soil with the crown level with the surrounding ground and give it room to sucker into a broad clump. Prune spent flower trusses right after bloom and remove a few of the oldest stems each year to keep the shrub vigorous. Expect fragrant lavender panicles in mid to late spring once the plant is a few years established.

planting.note: Plant a grafted or own-root shrub in spring or early fall.

directions: Site in full sun and neutral to alkaline soil, spacing plants to allow air movement around the canopy. Deadhead after flowering and thin older wood to encourage strong new shoots. The bicolor purple florets edged crisply in white open in mid spring and carry a rich fragrance.

planting.note: Set out in early spring or fall while dormant.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained bed with the root ball set even with the soil line. Remove faded blooms promptly and renew a few main canes over time to sustain flowering. Its full double lavender-pink trusses are heavily scented and appear in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery stock in spring or early autumn.

directions: Choose a full-sun site with fertile, well-drained soil and plant so the base sits level with the ground. Cut off spent panicles after bloom and thin crowded stems in late winter. This old French selection bears deep magenta-purple double florets with strong fragrance in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in fertile ground.

directions: Plant in full sun and neutral to alkaline soil, keeping the root crown at grade. Deadhead after flowering and open up the center of the shrub every few years by removing the oldest canes. Pure white double flowers open from cream buds in mid to late spring with a classic lilac scent.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun with good drainage, setting the plant no deeper than it grew in its container. Remove spent flowers and thin a portion of the older wood each season to keep it blooming freely. Large panicles of double lilac-blue florets appear in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall while dormant.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot with the crown at soil level and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Deadhead after bloom and prune only to remove old or crossing stems. Pink buds open to double, near-white florets with a delicate flush in mid spring, prized for their fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Site in full sun with fertile, well-drained soil, setting the base level with the ground. Remove faded trusses and thin the oldest canes to the ground periodically. This selection carries elongated panicles of single, deep wine-purple florets in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early autumn.

directions: Plant in full sun where the soft flower color can be appreciated, keeping the crown at grade in well-drained soil. Deadhead after flowering and prune lightly to shape. This unusual lilac opens pale primrose-yellow single florets in mid spring that fade toward cream.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained, near-neutral soil with the root ball set even with the surface. Trim off spent flowers and renew old wood over time. Known for some of the truest blue tones among lilacs, it bears single sky-blue florets in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping the crown at grade. Deadhead promptly after bloom and thin crowded stems as needed on this compact grower. Pink buds open to single lilac-blue florets on full panicles in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Set in full sun with fertile, well-drained soil and the crown level with the ground. Remove spent panicles and thin the oldest canes periodically to maintain vigor. Dense double florets of pale rosy-lilac open in mid spring with a sweet scent.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, setting the base even with grade. Deadhead after flowering and open the shrub's center by removing a few old stems each year. Purple buds open to double mauve-pink florets in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil with the crown at grade. Cut spent trusses after bloom and thin older wood as the shrub matures. Deep magenta-purple single florets open from dark buds in mid spring and fade paler with age.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Site in full sun and well-drained, near-neutral soil, keeping the crown level with the ground. Deadhead after flowering and remove aging canes over time. Large panicles of single, deep reddish-purple florets appear in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil with the root crown at grade. Remove faded flowers and thin crowded stems to keep the plant healthy. Soft pink single florets open in mid to late spring and are pleasantly fragrant.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, setting the crown level with the surface. Deadhead after bloom and thin the oldest wood periodically. Deep violet-purple single florets open in mid to late spring on this cold-hardy selection.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant this compact lilac in full sun and well-drained soil with the crown at grade. Deadhead after flowering and prune lightly to maintain its tidy form. Fragrant single blue florets open in mid spring on a shrub well suited to smaller gardens.

planting.note: Set out containerized plants in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing to allow the dense mounded form to fill in. It needs little pruning beyond removing spent blooms and any dead wood. Fragrant lavender-pink florets smother the compact shrub in late spring, often with light rebloom later.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil with the crown level with the ground. Deadhead after bloom and prune only to shape this naturally compact selection. Wine-red buds open to pink, spicy-scented florets in late spring.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping the crown at grade. Deadhead the first flush to encourage repeat flowering through the season. This tidy dwarf produces fragrant lavender-pink florets in late spring and reblooms sporadically into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil with the crown at grade, and shear the shrub lightly after the spring flush. This reblooming lilac flowers heavily in late spring, rests through early summer, then flushes again from midsummer to frost. Consistent deadheading and adequate water keep the later blooms coming.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing space for its upright rounded habit. It needs minimal pruning aside from deadheading and removing dead stems. Purple buds open to fragrant icy-lavender florets in late spring, and the foliage takes on burgundy tones in fall.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Site in full sun and well-drained soil, giving this arching hybrid room to spread gracefully. Deadhead after bloom and thin older stems to maintain form. Abundant fragrant lilac-purple florets cover the branches in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing space for its graceful arching branches. Remove spent panicles and thin crowded wood as the shrub matures. Fragrant lilac-red to purplish florets open densely along the stems in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil with the crown at grade. Deadhead after flowering and thin old canes over time. This early hybrid opens deep purple single florets from near-black buds a week or so ahead of common lilac in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping the crown level with the ground. Remove spent blooms and thin the oldest wood periodically. Soft pink single florets open early in the lilac season and carry a light fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Site in full sun and well-drained soil, giving this vigorous, very cold-hardy hybrid ample room. Deadhead after bloom and thin older canes as needed. Fragrant single lilac-blue florets open early in the season, ahead of most common lilacs.

planting.note: Plant this small tree in spring or fall while dormant.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, staking the young single or multi-stemmed tree its first year. It blooms reliably even when young and needs only light structural pruning after flowering. Large creamy-white panicles open in early summer, later than shrub lilacs, above glossy dark green leaves.

planting.note: Plant as a small tree in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil and stake the young tree until established. Prune lightly after bloom to develop a strong branch structure. Creamy-white fragrant panicles cover this compact, rounded tree lilac in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, giving this hardy Preston hybrid room to develop. Deadhead after bloom and thin older wood as needed. Rosy-pink florets open in late spring, after common lilacs finish, extending the lilac season.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil with the crown at grade. Remove spent flowers and thin the oldest canes over time. Clear pink florets open in late spring on this cold-hardy late-blooming hybrid.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Site in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing this vigorous hybrid room to spread. Deadhead after flowering and remove old wood periodically. Reddish-purple buds open to single rosy-lilac florets in late spring, well after common lilacs.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cold-winter regions.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, giving this dense, upright species plenty of room. It is very cold-hardy and needs little pruning beyond removing dead wood. Rosy-lilac to pale pink panicles open in late spring, after the common lilacs have faded.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun to light shade and well-drained soil, tolerating a range of conditions once established. It suckers less than common lilac and needs minimal pruning. Fragrant violet-purple florets open in late spring on upright, glossy-leaved stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing space for this large, vigorous species. Prune only to remove dead or crossing stems after flowering. Pale lilac to nearly white florets open in late spring in upright panicles above bold foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: Site in full sun and well-drained soil where late frosts are less likely, since it flowers very early. Deadhead after bloom and thin old wood as needed. Fragrant lilac-blue florets open ahead of most lilacs, and the broad leaves often color reddish in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil with room for its upright habit. Prune lightly after bloom to remove weak or dead stems. Distinctive drooping panicles of rosy-pink florets open in late spring, giving the plant its common name.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing room for its broad, spreading form. Deadhead the main flush to encourage a lighter repeat bloom. Fragrant rosy-pink florets open in late spring and often flush again in late summer on this small-leaved lilac.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, giving this graceful, fine-textured lilac room to arch. It tolerates heat better than common lilac and needs little pruning. Delicate lavender florets open along the finely dissected foliage in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping the crown level with the ground. Deadhead after flowering and thin older canes to keep the shrub open. Loose panicles of single reddish-purple florets open in mid spring on this vigorous old cultivar.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil with the crown at grade. Remove spent trusses and renew old wood over time. Slender panicles of double pinkish-mauve florets open in mid to late spring with a fine fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Site in full sun and well-drained soil, setting the base even with grade. Deadhead after bloom and thin the oldest wood periodically. Full panicles of single blue florets open from pinkish buds in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil with the crown level with the ground. Deadhead after flowering and remove aging canes over time. Double, deep reddish-purple florets open in mid to late spring on this reliable cultivar.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping the crown at grade. Deadhead after bloom and thin crowded stems as the shrub matures. Semi-double to double claret-red florets open in mid to late spring and hold their color well.

planting.note: Plant in spring where midday shade will protect the foliage from scorch.

directions: Give this gold-leaved form morning sun with afternoon shade to keep the bright foliage from bleaching or burning. Prune lightly after bloom to shape and to renew flowering wood. Fragrant white flowers open against the chartreuse leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with some afternoon shade to protect the cream leaf margins.

directions: Site in bright but not blistering light so the white leaf edges stay crisp. Remove any all-green reverting shoots as soon as they appear. Sweetly scented white flowers stand out against the green-and-cream foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this western native tolerates lean, dry soils once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water regularly the first year, then reduce watering as it settles in. Renew old wood by cutting a few of the eldest stems to the ground after flowering. Fragrant four-petaled white flowers cover the shrub in late spring, the state flower of Idaho.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, well-drained site; excellent for xeric gardens.

directions: This compact southwestern species wants sharp drainage and full sun. Water sparingly once established, as it resents wet feet. Small pineapple-scented white flowers appear along the fine-textured stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a vigorous native for naturalistic borders.

directions: Give this arching native room to spread and thin crowded stems after bloom. Named for its nearly scentless flowers, it makes up for it with generous bloom. Large white flowers open in late spring on gracefully bowing branches.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerant of a wide range of soils including clay.

directions: This tall, upright species has downy grayish undersides to its leaves. Prune out the oldest wood after flowering to keep it vigorous. Mildly fragrant white flowers appear in loose clusters in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or light shade for the best flowering.

directions: Set out in fertile, well-drained soil and prune one in four old stems after flowering. This arching shrub is prized for its scent and its distinctive flowers. Fragrant single white blooms flushed maroon-purple at the center appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact form well suited to small gardens and containers.

directions: This dwarf, mounded selection fits tight borders and large pots. Prune lightly after flowering just to shape it. Creamy double white flowers with a strong sweet fragrance smother the plant in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the heaviest bloom.

directions: Give this upright shrub room and renew it by removing older canes after bloom. It is one of the most popular double-flowered mock oranges. Very fragrant semi-double to double white flowers open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; bred for cold-climate hardiness.

directions: This cold-tough selection performs well in northern gardens. Cut back a portion of the oldest stems after flowering to keep it dense. Fragrant double white flowers cover the upright branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact double-flowered form for smaller spaces.

directions: Its neat, rounded habit suits foundation plantings and mixed borders. Prune only lightly after bloom to maintain the shape. Fragrant fully double white flowers appear along the stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a very compact form ideal for small gardens and pots.

directions: This dwarf selection stays low and tidy with little pruning. Site it where its heavy fragrance can be enjoyed up close. Double white flowers pack the compact branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a vigorous, large cultivar for the back of the border.

directions: Give this robust shrub plenty of space to reach its full size. Thin the oldest canes after flowering to keep growth strong. Large fragrant single white flowers open profusely in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted for its unusual quill-like petals.

directions: This distinctive selection carries flowers with narrow, rolled petals like quills. Prune after bloom to renew flowering wood. Fragrant double white blooms with their curious petals appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade to enjoy the variegated foliage.

directions: This form offers creamy-variegated leaves as well as fragrant flowers. Give it light afternoon shade to protect the leaf color. Single white blooms with an intense scent open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a graceful Lemoine hybrid for the mixed border.

directions: This arching shrub bears large flowers with a faint rosy blush at the base. Remove some of the oldest stems each year after flowering. Broad fragrant single white blooms open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a low, spreading form for the front of the border.

directions: This compact, arching shrub is one of the most fragrant mock oranges. Prune lightly after bloom to keep its graceful shape. Single white flowers stained purple toward the center open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the arching stems bow under the weight of bloom.

directions: This slender-stemmed Lemoine hybrid is smothered in flowers each summer. Thin the oldest wood after bloom to keep it vigorous. Small, intensely fragrant single white flowers cascade down the branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; blooms in dense clustered heads.

directions: This selection carries flowers in tight bouquet-like clusters. Prune after flowering to renew the blooming wood. Fragrant double white flowers gather in showy heads in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; bred for extra cold hardiness.

directions: This hardy selection reliably flowers in cold northern gardens. Cut out older canes after bloom to keep it dense and productive. Fragrant double white flowers open along the branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an upright, rounded form.

directions: This tidy, rounded shrub flowers heavily along its upright stems. Prune after bloom, removing the oldest wood at the base. Single white fragrant flowers cover the plant in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact form good for containers and small beds.

directions: This small, dense shrub blooms so heavily the branches seem to drip with flowers. Prune lightly after bloom to maintain its neat form. Fragrant single white flowers smother the plant in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its name means silver ball for the round double flowers.

directions: This heritage French selection bears ball-shaped fully double flowers. Renew a few old stems each year after flowering. Rounded, moderately fragrant double white blooms open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact Lemoine hybrid.

directions: This low, bushy hybrid produces masses of small fragrant flowers. Prune after bloom to shape and to encourage new flowering shoots. Very fragrant single white flowers cover the plant in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a strongly double-flowered upright form.

directions: This upright shrub is prized for its densely double flowers. Thin the oldest canes after bloom to keep it vigorous. Clusters of fragrant double white flowers weigh down the branch tips in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted for its pronounced rose-pink flower centers.

directions: This selection stands out for the strong rosy blotch at each flower's heart. Prune after flowering to renew the blooming wood. Fragrant single white flowers with rose-pink centers open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an extremely cold-hardy selection from the northern plains.

directions: This exceptionally hardy native selection thrives where other mock oranges freeze out. Water regularly to establish, then reduce once settled. Fragrant white flowers blanket the upright shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a double-flowered form of the western native.

directions: This vigorous double form of Lewis's mock orange tolerates dry sites once established. Thin the oldest stems after flowering. Very fragrant double white flowers cover the shrub in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a drought-tolerant selection for the Great Plains.

directions: This tough native selection handles heat, cold, and dry soil. Establish with regular water, then let it fend for itself. Single white fragrant flowers cover the plant in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a classic double white for the shrub border.

directions: This upright shrub produces abundant fully double flowers. Prune after flowering, taking out the oldest canes at the base. Fragrant double white blooms cover the branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a cold-hardy East Asian species.

directions: This hardy species from northeastern Asia tolerates severe winter cold. Prune out old wood after flowering to renew the shrub. Fragrant white flowers open in early summer, among the earliest of the mock oranges.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a graceful Japanese species.

directions: This slender Japanese species has an airy, arching habit. Thin the oldest stems after bloom to keep growth open. Lightly fragrant white flowers appear in loose clusters in early summer.

planting.note: Plant after the last hard frost in mild-winter regions; can be trained as a scrambling climber.

directions: This tender evergreen species has a lax, semi-climbing habit and can be trained on a support. Protect from hard frost and prune after bloom to control its size. Very fragrant creamy-white flowers open over a long season in warm climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted for a long rebloom period.

directions: This selection is valued for reblooming through summer rather than a single flush. Deadhead and prune lightly to encourage repeat flowering. Fragrant double white flowers appear in early summer and again later in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a modern selection with a graceful arching form.

directions: This improved selection forms a fountain of arching stems that bow with bloom. Prune out the oldest wood after flowering. Fragrant single white flowers line the arching branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact, upright modern selection.

directions: This tidy, upright selection suits smaller gardens and mixed borders. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain its neat habit. Fragrant white flowers cover the compact plant in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sharply drained spot.

directions: This upright, fine-textured selection wants full sun and excellent drainage. Water sparingly once established, as it dislikes wet soil. Small, sweetly pineapple-scented white flowers appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact, richly scented selection.

directions: This compact shrub is grown as much for its perfume as its flowers. Site it near a path or seating area to enjoy the scent. Fragrant white flowers open along the branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a very compact double form for small spaces.

directions: This dwarf form stays low and needs almost no pruning. Site it at the front of a border where its fragrance carries. Double white flowers pack the small branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a very cold-hardy species from the Russian Far East.

directions: This hardy species tolerates deep winter cold and light shade. Thin the oldest canes after flowering to keep it vigorous. Lightly fragrant white flowers open in late spring, earlier than most mock oranges.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hybrid group grown for its purple-blotched flowers.

directions: This hybrid group is grown for single flowers marked with purple at the center. Prune after bloom, removing some of the oldest stems. Fragrant white flowers with purple basal blotches open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a classic single-flowered form of the sweet mock orange.

directions: This vigorous selection carries the strong scent of the species. Renew old wood by cutting a few eldest canes after flowering. Fragrant single white flowers cover the tall shrub in early summer.

planting.note: Set out balled or potted plants in early spring or fall while the soil is workable.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the root ball and twice as wide, keeping the crown level with the surrounding soil. Water deeply through the first summer and mulch to hold moisture around the shallow roots. Flat white lacecap flowers open in late spring, followed by clusters of glossy red fruit that persist into winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in moist, well-drained ground.

directions: Position the shrub with its crown at soil level and firm the backfill to remove air pockets. Prune out the oldest stems right after flowering, since blooms form on the previous year's wood. Round clusters of sterile white florets swell like snowballs in mid to late spring, aging from pale green to pure white.

planting.note: Set out in spring in rich, evenly moist soil with a little afternoon shade in hot regions.

directions: Plant so the root ball sits level with the grade and mulch lightly to keep the roots cool. Avoid heavy pruning, as the tiered branching pattern is part of the plant's charm. Rounded snowball clusters of white florets cover the horizontal branches in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the tiered horizontal branches have room to spread.

directions: Give this shrub wide, open space so the layered branches can show their flat, tabletop form. Keep the soil evenly moist and mulched, and prune only to remove dead wood. Rows of white lacecap flowers line the upper sides of the branches in late spring, giving the tiers a snow-dusted look.

planting.note: Set out in spring in moist, well-drained soil with room to spread wide.

directions: Plant with the crown at grade and allow generous width, since this selection grows broader than tall. Water through dry spells and mulch to conserve moisture. Unusually large white lacecap flowers smother the horizontal branches in spring, followed by red fruit that ripens to black.

planting.note: Plant near a doorway or path in spring so the fragrance can be enjoyed.

directions: Set the root ball level with the soil surface and water in well. Prune just after bloom to shape, since flowers form on old wood. Pink buds open to white, powerfully clove-scented flower clusters in mid spring, one of the most fragrant of all viburnums.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot where its perfume can be appreciated up close.

directions: Plant with the crown at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch the root zone. Shape lightly right after flowering to preserve next year's buds. Deep red buds open to fragrant white blooms flushed pink in mid spring, with foliage turning wine-red in autumn.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a sunny, sheltered spot with good drainage.

directions: Dig the planting hole twice the width of the root ball and set the crown at soil level. Water regularly the first season and prune only after bloom. Large rounded clusters of fragrant white flowers open from pink buds in mid spring, combining snowball form with rich perfume.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in average, well-drained garden soil.

directions: Position the shrub with its crown even with the grade and firm the soil around the roots. Water deeply while establishing and mulch to retain moisture. Domed clusters of spicy-scented white flowers open from pink buds in mid spring above semi-evergreen leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the best flower and fall color.

directions: Set the root ball level with the soil line and water in thoroughly. Prune lightly after flowering to keep a rounded shape. Red flower buds open to white, clove-scented blossoms in mid spring, and the disease-resistant foliage turns orange-red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; set two or more nearby to improve fruit set.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and pair with another arrowwood for cross-pollination and heavier fruit. Water through the first year and mulch the base. White spring flowers give way to clusters of intensely blue berries in late summer on this compact selection.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a hardy hedge or screen.

directions: Set the root ball even with the grade and water deeply while it establishes. This tough selection shrugs off cold, heat, and clay and makes an excellent informal screen. Glossy dark leaves back flat white flower clusters in early summer, followed by blue-black fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist to wet soil, tolerating boggy sites.

directions: Choose a moist or even wet spot and set the crown at soil level. This native thrives near ponds and rain gardens with little intervention. Creamy white flowers open in early summer, followed by berries that shift from pink to blue and glossy autumn foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; add a second witherod nearby for good berry set.

directions: Set the root ball level with the grade in moist, acidic soil and pair with another clone for cross-pollination. Water freely, as this shrub loves damp ground. White spring flowers yield showy fruit that ripens from pink to deep blue, and glossy leaves turn burgundy in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist soil; sets fruit well even planted alone.

directions: Plant with the crown at grade in consistently moist ground and mulch to hold water. This selection fruits reliably, though a companion witherod increases the display. Pink and blue berries mix in the same cluster in early autumn above glossy, wine-colored leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil in cold regions.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and water through the first summer. Extremely cold-hardy, this native needs little care and tolerates part shade. Flat white lacecap flowers open in late spring, followed by edible tart red fruit that persists and feeds birds through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground for its heavy fruit crop.

directions: Plant with the crown at grade and keep the soil evenly moist while roots establish. Grown for its abundant fruit, which is useful in jelly and prized by wildlife. White spring flowers give way to red berries in late summer, and the foliage colors well in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in almost any soil, wet or dry.

directions: Set the root ball level with the grade and water well the first season. Highly adaptable, this native grows as a large shrub or small tree and takes drought and shade in stride. White flower clusters open in mid spring, followed by blue-black edible fruit and red-purple autumn leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in mild regions as a compact hedge or foundation shrub.

directions: Plant with the crown level and water in well, providing good drainage. This dense, compact selection responds well to light shearing for a formal look. Pink buds open to clusters of white flowers over a long winter-to-spring season, followed by blue fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered, well-drained spot.

directions: Set the root ball at grade and water while establishing, then keep evenly moist. This compact form takes clipping well and suits containers or low hedges. Deep pink buds open to white flowers through the cooler months, followed by dark metallic-blue berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered spot where winter bloom is visible.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and water through the first year to establish. Plant near a walk or entry so the winter fragrance can be enjoyed. Clusters of pink, sweetly scented flowers open on bare stems from late autumn through late winter during mild spells.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a protected site for early bloom.

directions: Position the shrub with its crown at grade and water deeply while establishing. Site it out of harsh wind so the earliest flowers are not damaged. Pink buds open to fragrant white flower clusters on leafless branches in late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil as an evergreen screen.

directions: Set the root ball level with the grade and water regularly the first season. This fast-growing evergreen makes a dense hedge and tolerates cold better than most broadleaf evergreens. Creamy white flowers open from pink buds in mid spring above glossy, wrinkled dark green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shady spot out of winter wind.

directions: Set the crown at grade and shelter this shrub from drying winter wind, which can scorch the large leaves. Plant with another leatherleaf to encourage fruit. Flat clusters of yellowish-white flowers open in late spring, followed by red fruit ripening to black.

planting.note: Plant in spring in mild regions as a low evergreen groundcover shrub.

directions: Set the root ball level with the soil in a partly shaded, well-drained spot. Plant male and female forms together to obtain the ornamental fruit. Small white flowers appear in late spring above bold, deeply veined leaves, followed by striking turquoise-blue berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered site with room to grow large.

directions: Give this big shrub ample space and set the crown level with the grade. Water deeply through dry spells and prune after bloom to manage size. Enormous rounded clusters of sterile florets open lime-green and mature to pure white in mid spring, sometimes reblooming in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water while establishing. Prized for its foliage, which emerges maroon-red and matures to green. Flat lacecap flowers with white outer florets and pink-tinged fertile centers open in late spring above the dark young leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grow with lower shrubs to hide its leggy base.

directions: Set the root ball level with the soil and water deeply the first season. The base grows open and leggy, so underplant with lower shrubs or perennials. White spring flowers are modest, but heavy clusters of brilliant orange-red fruit weigh down the branches in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in dry to average soil, including alkaline sites.

directions: Set the root ball level with the grade and water while establishing, after which it tolerates drought. This large, adaptable shrub handles poor and alkaline soils well. Flat white flower clusters open in late spring, followed by fruit that ripens from red to black.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in average, well-drained soil as a compact hedge.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water through the first season. This dense, compact selection resists bacterial leaf spot and holds its rounded shape. White flower clusters open in late spring, and the orange-red fruit stays colorful for several weeks before turning black.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, acidic soil, tolerating wet ground.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in a moist, acidic site and water while establishing. This native takes wet ground and part shade in stride. Creamy flowers open in early summer, followed by fruit that turns green, pink, red, and finally blue on the same cluster.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the fragrance can be enjoyed near a path.

directions: Set the root ball level with the grade and water well the first season. This rounded hybrid is more heat-tolerant and disease-resistant than its Koreanspice parent. Pink buds open to fragrant white flower clusters in mid spring, with wine-red fall foliage to follow.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in average, well-drained soil for its fall fruit.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply while establishing. Grown mainly for its heavy autumn fruit display, best with another plant nearby for pollination. White flower clusters open in late spring, followed by dense clusters of bright red berries and reddish fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; group plants for best fruit set.

directions: Set the root ball level with the grade and pair with another linden viburnum for heavy fruiting. Water while establishing and mulch the root zone. Flat white flower clusters open in late spring, followed by profuse bright red berries that last well into winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; add another linden viburnum nearby for the best berry crop.

directions: Set the crown at grade and plant with a compatible pollinator for maximum fruit. Water deeply through the first summer and mulch. White spring flowers give way to a heavy, cold-hardy display of glossy red berries that persist into winter on this compact selection.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained soil with a bit of afternoon shade in the South.

directions: Set the root ball level with the grade and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. More upright and compact than older doublefiles, it fits smaller gardens. White lacecap flowers appear along the horizontal branches in late spring and keep coming sporadically through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring with room to reach large size, or train to a single trunk.

directions: Set the crown level with the grade and water deeply while it establishes. This bold shrub can be limbed up into a small tree with striking coarse foliage. Creamy flower clusters open in late spring, followed by pink-to-red fruit on red stalks that darkens to black.

planting.note: Plant in spring in the Southeast as an evergreen hedge or small tree.

directions: Set the root ball at grade and water regularly until established. This adaptable Southern native takes shearing well and suits formal hedges or a limbed-up small tree. Small white flowers cover the fine evergreen foliage in early spring, followed by fruit that ripens to black.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil as a dense semi-evergreen screen.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water deeply while establishing. This dense, rounded hybrid resists leaf spot and holds its leathery leaves through mild winters. Yellowish-white flower clusters open in late spring, followed by red fruit that ripens to black.

planting.note: Plant in spring in the South as a fast, dense evergreen screen.

directions: Set the root ball level with the grade and water regularly through the first year. This vigorous evergreen quickly forms a tall privacy screen with lustrous dark leaves. Fragrant white flower clusters open in spring, followed by showy red fruit in warm climates.

planting.note: Set young plants out in spring after the last hard frost has passed.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at the same depth the shrub grew in its container and water regularly until established. Cut the whole plant back hard to about a foot in late winter to encourage strong new flowering wood. Long fragrant flower spikes open from midsummer into fall and draw butterflies; snip spent spikes to keep new blooms coming.

planting.note: Set out in spring after the danger of hard frost.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and avoid soggy winter soil. Shear the framework down low in late winter so vigorous new shoots carry the flowers. Large magenta-red panicles open in summer and are a magnet for pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost date.

directions: Choose a sunny, well-drained site and water through the first summer as roots establish. Cut back near the base in late winter to renew the flowering stems. Pure white spikes with yellow throats bloom from mid-season; deadheading keeps the show fresh.

planting.note: Transplant outdoors in spring once frosts have finished.

directions: Plant level with the surrounding soil in a hot, sunny bed with sharp drainage. Prune hard in early spring to build a tidy shape and heavy flowering. Violet-blue spikes with orange eyes open through summer and attract clouds of butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the ground warms.

directions: Site in full sun with well-drained soil and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Cut stems back to a low framework in late winter for the biggest flower trusses. Long, tapered bright pink spikes bloom all summer and are richly scented.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frosts end.

directions: Plant this compact selection in a sunny, sharply drained spot and keep winter soil on the dry side. Prune to a low framework in early spring to keep the fine-textured growth neat. Slender lavender-blue spikes over narrow gray-green leaves flower throughout summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the danger of frost passes.

directions: Give it full sun and free-draining soil, watering only until established. Shear back hard in late winter to renew the arching, small-leaved stems. Rich purple flower spikes open from summer into autumn and lure many butterflies.

planting.note: Plant outdoors in spring after the final frost.

directions: Plant in a warm sunny border with well-drained soil and room to spread. Prune down to a low framework each late winter for vigorous flowering shoots. Long, slim, deep violet spikes appear through summer and are heavily fragrant.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frosts have finished.

directions: Allow generous space, as this vigorous selection makes a large, arching bush; plant in full sun with good drainage. Cut back hard in late winter to control size and boost bloom. Unusual branched, many-fingered magenta panicles smother the plant in summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and mulch to keep roots cool. Renew the bush by cutting low in early spring. Large lilac-pink spikes bloom from midsummer and draw abundant pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Set into a sunny, well-drained bed and water only until the roots take hold. Prune to a low framework in late winter for compact, floriferous growth. Rich blue-violet spikes with orange centers open through summer.

common_name: Lo & Behold Blue Chip Butterfly Bush

planting.note: Plant this dwarf in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this mound-forming dwarf in full sun and well-drained soil, ideal at the front of a border or in a container. It stays tidy and needs only a light spring trim rather than hard pruning. Continuous blue-purple spikes bloom from early summer to frost without deadheading.

common_name: Lo & Behold Lilac Chip Butterfly Bush

planting.note: Set out in spring after frosts end.

directions: Plant this very compact selection in a sunny, well-drained spot where its low mound can spread. Trim lightly in spring to shape; no hard cutback is needed. Soft lilac-pink spikes flower nonstop from summer through fall.

common_name: Lo & Behold Ice Chip Butterfly Bush

planting.note: Plant in spring after the danger of frost.

directions: Use this low, spreading dwarf as a groundcover-like edging in full sun and free-draining soil. A light spring haircut keeps it neat; skip the hard pruning full-size types need. White spikes with a faint yellow eye bloom from early summer into autumn.

common_name: Lo & Behold Pink Micro Chip Butterfly Bush

planting.note: Plant in spring once frosts finish.

directions: Tuck this miniature into containers or the border front in full sun with good drainage. Only a light spring trim is required to keep it compact. Small soft-pink spikes flower freely from summer to frost and draw butterflies to small spaces.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost date.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained bed and water through the first season only. Cut back to a low framework in late winter for the richest color and strong reblooming. Deep sangria-red spikes, among the darkest reds available, flower all summer.

planting.note: Plant outdoors in spring after frosts.

directions: Give it full sun and sharp drainage for the best flower color. Prune hard in early spring to shape a compact, dense bush. Vivid magenta-pink spikes appear from midsummer and keep coming with light deadheading.

planting.note: Set out in spring once frost risk ends.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing to allow good airflow. Cut low in late winter to renew flowering wood. Saturated deep violet spikes bloom densely from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Choose a sunny, freely draining site and water only to establish. Prune to a low framework in early spring for the fullest flowering. Fragrant creamy-white spikes bloom from summer to frost and attract many butterflies.

planting.note: Plant this compact type in spring after frosts.

directions: Plant this short, thick-stemmed dwarf in full sun with good drainage; its sturdy stems give better winter hardiness than most small types. A light spring trim is all it needs to stay neat. Full-size true-blue spikes with orange eyes bloom on a compact frame from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Set into a sunny, well-drained bed where the compact mound has room to fill in. Trim lightly in spring to tidy the thick stems. Rich purple-pink spikes bloom on short growth from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant outdoors in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun and freely draining soil, spacing for a low, rounded habit. Give only a light shaping cut in spring. Bright pink spikes with orange throats bloom on a dwarf frame all summer long.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frosts have ended.

directions: Plant this stocky dwarf in full sun with good drainage for reliable performance. Only a light spring trim is needed to keep it compact. Pure white spikes with tiny yellow eyes bloom on short stems from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost date.

directions: This patio-sized dwarf thrives in full sun and containers with free-draining mix. Cut back by about half in early spring to keep it bushy. Soft blue spikes bloom from summer to frost; snip spent flowers to encourage more.
